I want first-person passages felt from inside the experience, where you can hear the moment the held-at-arm's-length watching gives way to being drawn in. Favour the strange, specific, friction-bearing accounts — the writer who resisted the closeness, or was surprised by it, or didn't fully want it — over smooth, broadly relatable epiphanies. For every passage give the author, the book title, and enough of a locator (chapter, edition, or page) that I can find it myself; quote the actual words. Only return genuine matches to this exact shape of movement. Three real passages beat ten loose ones, and fewer is fine if fewer truly fit. If you cannot find memoirs that specifically document this turn from dissecting-at-a-distance to feeling-with, say so plainly and do not invent, pad, compose, or force a count. Read these in their own context. Then ask yourself: does this seem real for me — and if not, why not?
